title: The Promise
original_prose:
"If I gave you time to change my mind, I'd try to leave all the past behind...

But I just might need you like I've never done before."

— Sturgill Simpson, The Promise

####

[PRIORITY RECALL – SYNRGY ONE CORE CITADEL – INTERNAL SECURE COMM]FROM: CENTRAL STRATEGIC AUTHORITYTO: ALL ACTIVE FIELD COMMANDS

RE: INSURRECTION CASCADE

— Full withdrawal of exterior assets is now authorized.— Activate all WRAITH-class compliance vessels.— Reinforce Core perimeter and contain internal Spark resonance events.— Public executions will proceed on looped broadcast. Optics remain viable.

“Fear is clarity. Pain is proof. The Protocol remains divine.”



####

The void isn’t smaller. It’s still infinite.

There’s just less in it now.

Like a cathedral whose icons have all been stripped. No saints. No stained glass. Just echoes of prayers no one believes in anymore. The stone beneath us is cracked, barely wide enough for both of us to sit. Edges crumble with every breath.

I don’t know how long we’ve been here.

The sword rests beside me. Quiet. Still humming, but only faintly. Not out of loyalty. Not even habit. Just inertia.

He’s across from me. The Child.

Still barefoot. Legs crossed. Shoulders square. But older, somehow. The shape of him sharper. Not grown—just forming.

We don’t speak.

There’s nothing to say. Not yet.

The dark around us flickers. Not like stars. Like power dying. A system collapsing pixel by pixel.

Eventually:

"You lived a lifetime of love in a few days."

I nod. Because it’s true.

The silence after isn’t cruel. It’s reverent. Like breath held at the edge of something sacred.

He doesn’t look at me when he speaks next. Just watches the edge of the rock. Where the black waits.

"It gets worse, doesn’t it?"

Another nod.

A hairline crack splits the stone beneath me. The sword hums once. Like it heard something it didn’t like.

We stay like that.

Not in conversation.

Just in the last moment before the flood.

Waiting.



####

The walls are too white.

Not sterile. Not clean. Just blank. A kind of engineered forgetting. There’s no window. No door frame. Just one seamless rectangle, lit from nowhere.

And the screen.

The screen doesn’t blink. It just exists. Always on. Always playing. The angle never changes. Mid-shot. Kat in a restraint rig. Upright. Barefoot. Bleeding. Bruised across the collarbone like someone forgot she was human before they strapped her down.

I don’t look away.

Not because I’m strong. Because I can’t.

There’s a voice in the wall. No speaker. No origin. Just vibration in the bones.

"Hello, Lieutenant.

Welcome home.

I must say, watching your recent adventures has been quite the spectacle. The desert monologues. The canyon romance. The song. Very moving. Almost made me believe in free will again.

You know, when you abandoned your post, I assumed you were simply having a breakdown. An expected side effect of prolonged Spark proximity. Nothing the Protocol couldn’t resolve with correctional therapy and a decent neural scrape.

But then you started making choices. Real ones. Wild ones.

But you led us to the last rebel stronghold. You paraded your insubordination in front of their children like it was a lesson worth teaching. And then, like a loyal dog trying to reclaim its dignity, you brought us a gift.

The heretic you call Kat.

She was a myth, you know. A ghost in the system. Spark spikes. Static on the rails. No one believed she was still alive.

Until you gave her back to us.

So thank you.

Thank you for finishing what the WRAITHs could not. Thank you for proving, once and for all, that love is a weapon forged by the weak to justify their own delusion.

And thank you, Lieutenant, for returning to us just in time to see what clarity truly looks like."

Voss. I recognize the rasp. The elegance, rotted.

"Watch her, Lieutenant.

Watch how we strip away the lie she tried to sell your species. This charming delusion that will can triumph over structure. That choice matters more than order.

There are men. There are women. There is nothing in between."

He pauses, like he wants me to absorb it—not just the words, but the belief behind them. The certainty.

"I made women—real women—to serve men. To inspire them. To soften them. To surrender. That’s their function in the system. In my system.

What this thing is… was… It doesn’t fit. And that, Lieutenant, is the definition of corruption.

Do you know what we do with corrupted files?"

The screen zooms in.

"We purge them."

His voice never rises. Never wavers. Just slides along my spine like cold oil.

"Everything has its place. Every Spark, every flesh-vector, every name. What you call love is noise. This heretic was noise. Dangerous. Viral. And now—thanks to your little detour—we’ve reclaimed the narrative.

Consider it a kindness that we broadcast its correction. The world needed the reminder."

The camera tightens. I can’t look away.

"Don’t flinch. You broke the order. This is what it costs."

Kat’s head lifts. Just barely. One eye swollen. One lip split.

She doesn’t cry.

They start the removal.

No ceremony. No sedation. Just SynRgy doctrine: reclaim the Spark, discard the delusion.

I fall to my knees.

Not dramatic. Not heroic. Just… empty. Like something inside me collapsed all at once and didn’t leave anything behind to brace the fall.

"Please."

My voice doesn’t even echo. It just lands. Dead. Soft.

"Please, you don’t have to do this. You’ve made your point. You’ve won. Just… let her go."

I don’t know who I’m begging.

The system. The gods. The man in the wall.

"She is not a threat anymore. Please. I’ll do anything."

I press my forehead to the floor. Not because I’m praying. Because I can’t hold my own head up.

"Let her go."

The laugh that answers me is slow. Gentle. Paternal.

"No, my son."

Voss breathes the words like he’s tucking me into bed.

"We only let it go the first time so you could lead us to their nest. Did you really think we’d allow you to commit such blasphemy again?"

He lets that hang. Then:

"You were always the bait. Ever since that night in Kansas. Tell me, Lieutenant, did you know it was a tranny then? Did you know what sin you were committing? Did you know it had a cock then? Maybe that's what excited you, Lieutenant?"

The screen sharpens. The blades descend.

I watch them cut her.

Scalp first. The symbol of selfhood. Then the breasts—unmade with the same clinical precision as a tumor. Then lower.

She doesn’t scream.

But I do, not that it does any good.

They do it slow. For the cameras. For the loop.

Voss hums.

"What do you think makes a woman, Lieutenant?

Long hair? Tits? A pleasant voice on the correct frequency?

Do you think that’s all I asked of the women I created?"

The screen cuts briefly to static. Then her again. Kat. Strapped in. Skin glinting with blood. The camera zooms in—not on her eyes, but between her legs. A bloody mess. How is she not screaming?

"This thing doesn’t even have a vagina, Lieutenant. Did you know that? Of course you knew that when you rutted around with it like an animal."

This thing could never bear a child.

And that—that—is the only real thing I ever asked of the women I made.

Bring us children to power the grid. Bring us children to worship me. To sing my name in the data. To feel awe when the lights come on. To kneel."

His voice cracks—not with weakness. With ecstasy.

"It could never give me that. So what is it, really? Just a kink with a knife. Just signal noise in a dress.

A failed prayer."

He breathes like something holy just passed through him.

"So yes, Lieutenant. We are going to unmake her."

He almost sounds sad.

"Because the world has no room left for anything that doesn’t fit the diagram I drew in the dark."

My hand hits the wall. Once. Again. Again. Bones snap. Skin splits. Doesn’t matter. Not enough.

The screen stutters.

Kat turns her head. Finds the lens. Finds me.

She smiles.

"He’s coming," she says. Voice like gravel. Like prayer. "And he won’t ask for forgiveness."

Voss again:

"Do you think she means you, Lieutenant? Surely she doesn't think you are coming for her? It's far too late for heroics. The only thing left, for you, is to beg for forgiveness before your own death."

Two technicians enter the frame.

They unstrap it from the chair—not gently, not cruelly. Just efficiently. Like it’s a faulty battery. A broken tool.

They carry it outside.

The camera follows. Shaky. Handheld. Or maybe the building is shaking. The city above is burning. Smoke threads the corners of the feed.

They lash it to a blackened telephone pole in the middle of a pyre. Old wood. Synthetic accelerants. There’s no audience. Just one lens. Just me.

I reach for the screen.

"Please," I whisper. "Please don’t."

My throat cracks.

"You’ve taken everything from her. Please. Just let her."

Laughter, again. Richer this time. Like a sermon about to crescendo.

"No, son. We burn sinners."

The flame hits before I can scream again.

She doesn’t beg. Doesn’t struggle.

"REMEMBER ME!"

The fire roars. The image shakes. The signal fractures.

I scream too.

And the world begins to end.

The screen loops.

But it doesn’t.

Just for a moment—

The image fractures. Flickers. Kat gone. Replaced by faces. Hundreds. Layered. Screaming. Weeping. One child’s voice:

"I remember the fire."

The screen hisses. The voice cuts out. Then back.

"Ignore it," Voss says. "Sometimes the recycled ones... echo."

He doesn’t sound certain. It's the first time I've heard doubt in his voice.

I lean forward. Not because I expect to touch anything. Because I have to be closer. The screen isn’t just glass anymore. It breathes.

Then:

A burst. Distant. Structural. Like something gave way.

The lights dim. Flicker. Not like a power outage. Like hesitation.

Sirens whine. Cut off. Somewhere above, metal screams.

The screen shuts off.

The wall breathes.

The lock disengages.

The door slides open.

No alarm. No command. Just entropy.

"I'm coming for you, Voss."
